var-2019-kvant2-XYZ-009.pitfall: Den vanligaste fûÊllan ûÊr att tro att radien ûÊr halva hypotenusan i en likbent rûÊtvinklig triangel. Botemedlet: hypotenusan ûÊr inte tangent hûÊr ã kateterna ûÊr. MûÊt avstûËndet frûËn medelpunkten till de korrekta tangentlinjerna.
var-2019-kvant2-XYZ-009.solution_path: Likbent rûÊtvinklig triangel med area 50 har katet 10 (eftersom (1/2)ôñaôý = 50 ã a = 10). Cirkelns medelpunkt pûË hypotenusans mittpunkt har radie 5 (avstûËnd till kateterna). Omkrets = 2ür = 10ü. Svaret ûÊr A.

steps.text: Triangel ABC ûÊr LIKBENT och har HYPOTENUSAN AC ã alltsûË ûÊr den rûÊtvinklig vid B. En cirkel har medelpunkt pûË mitten av AC och tangerar bûËde AB och BC. Triangelarea = 50 cmôý. Hitta cirkelns omkrets.
steps.title: FûÑrstûË problemet

steps.text: En rûÊtvinklig triangel dûÊr de tvûË kateterna ûÊr LIKA lûËnga. Vinkeln vid den rûÊta ûÊr 90ô¯, de andra tvûË ûÊr 45ô¯ var.
steps.title: Vad betyder 'likbent rûÊtvinklig triangel'?

steps.text: Med katet a: arean = (1/2)ôñaôña = aôý/2 = 50, sûË aôý = 100 ã a = 10 cm. BûËda kateter ûÊr 10 cm.
steps.title: RûÊkna ut sidan

steps.text: SûÊtt B = (0, 0), A = (10, 0), C = (0, 10). DûË AB ligger pûË x-axeln, BC pûË y-axeln, AC ûÊr hypotenusan.
steps.title: Placera triangeln

steps.text: Cirkelns medelpunkt ûÊr (5, 5) och den TANGERAR bûËde AB (x-axeln) och BC (y-axeln). AvstûËndet frûËn (5, 5) till x-axeln (y = 0) ûÊr 5. AvstûËndet till y-axeln ûÊr 5. BûËda lika ã alltsûË ûÊr radien r = 5 cm.
steps.title: Cirkelns radie

steps.text: En cirkel TANGERAR en linje nûÊr den nuddar linjen i exakt EN punkt. Det intrûÊffar nûÊr avstûËndet frûËn cirkelmedelpunkten till linjen ûÊr lika med RADIEN.
steps.title: Vad ûÊr 'tangera'?

steps.text: Omkretsen av en cirkel ûÊr Ÿ2\pi rŸ.
steps.title: Omkretsformeln

steps.text: Omkrets = 2ü ôñ 5 = 10ü cm.
steps.title: RûÊkna omkretsen

steps.text: Hypotenusan AC har lûÊngd Ÿ\sqrt{10^2 + 10^2} = \sqrt{200} = 10\sqrt{2}Ÿ cm ã 14,1 cm. Cirkeln har diameter 10 (= 2ôñ5) ã passar bra inom triangeln.
steps.title: Sanity-check mot hypotenusan

steps.text: Svaret ûÊr A ã omkrets 10ü cm. Insikten i en mening: fûÑr en cirkel med medelpunkt pûË mitten av hypotenusan som tangerar kateterna, ûÊr RADIEN = halva katetlûÊngden (eftersom kateten ûÊr vinkelrûÊt axel och avstûËndet till axeln ûÊr just halva katetlûÊngden).
steps.title: Slutsats

var-2019-kvant2-XYZ-009.technique: Inskriven cirkel i rûÊtvinklig triangel: nûÊr cirkelns medelpunkt ligger pûË en sida (typ mittpunkten av hypotenusan) och tangerar de andra tvûË, ûÊr radien = avstûËndet frûËn medelpunkten till tangentlinjen. Triggern: 'cirkel tangerar kateter, medelpunkt pûË hypotenusan' ã koordinatsûÊtt och mûÊt avstûËnd.

pregrade_tactic.move: Cirkelns radie till en tangenslinje ûÊr vinkelrûÊt mot linjen ã koordinatsûÊtt centrum och tangenspunkt fûÑr att rûÊkna radien.

distractors.why_tempting: Det ûÊr frestande att tro 'x + y ûÊr jûÊmnt eftersom siffrorna byts'.
distractors.why_wrong: Steg 9: 34 + 43 = 77 ûÊr UDDA. Delbart med 2 ûÊr inte garanterat ã beror pûË siffrorna.

distractors.why_tempting: MûËnga tûÊnker 'delbar med 3 om siffersumman ûÊr delbar med 3' utan att inse att summan inte alltid ûÊr delbar med 3.
distractors.why_wrong: Steg 9: x + y = 11ôñ(a+b). Delbart med 3 krûÊver att (a+b) ûÊr delbart med 3 ã sant ibland, inte alltid. FûÑr 34 + 43 = 77: 7+7 = 14, inte delbart med 3.

distractors.why_tempting: Snabbsvar ûÊr ofta '5 om siffrorna ûÊr speciella'.
distractors.why_wrong: Steg 9: 34 + 43 = 77, inte delbart med 5. Delbarhet med 5 krûÊver att sista siffran ûÊr 0 eller 5 ã inte garanterat.

var-2019-kvant2-XYZ-010.pitfall: Den vanligaste fûÊllan ûÊr att tro att alla smûË primtal ûÊr delare. Botemedlet: testa MED KONKRETA TAL (34 + 43 = 77, 25 + 52 = 77, ...) och se vilket delningsmûÑnster som ALLTID stûÊmmer.
var-2019-kvant2-XYZ-010.solution_path: x = 10a + b, y = 10b + a, sûË x + y = 11a + 11b = 11(a + b). Alltid delbart med 11. Svaret ûÊr D.

steps.text: x och y ûÊr tvûË-siffriga heltal med samma siffror i omvûÊnd ordning (typ 12 och 21, eller 34 och 43). Hitta talet som x + y ALLTID ûÊr delbart med.
steps.title: FûÑrstûË problemet

steps.text: Ett tal mellan 10 och 99. Det har tvûË siffror: tiotalssiffran och entalssiffran. Talet 34 har tiotal 3 och ental 4.
steps.title: Vad ûÊr ett tvûËsiffrigt heltal?

steps.text: Om x har tiotalssiffra a och entalssiffra b, dûË ûÊr x = 10a + b. (Tiotalet rûÊknas 10 gûËnger, entalet 1 gûËng.)
steps.title: Skriv x algebraiskt

steps.text: Y ûÊr x med siffrorna omkastade, alltsûË tiotal b och ental a: y = 10b + a.
steps.title: Skriv y algebraiskt

steps.text: 11a + 11b = 11(a + b). Faktorn 11 ûÊr gemensam.
steps.title: Faktorisera

steps.text: x + y = 11 ôñ (a + b). AlltsûË ûÊr x + y ALLTID en multipel av 11 ã DELBART MED 11.
steps.title: Vad betyder detta?

steps.text: x = 34, y = 43: x + y = 77. ûr 77 delbart med 2? Nej (77 ûÊr udda). ûr 77 delbart med 3? Nej (7+7=14, inte delbart med 3). ûr 77 delbart med 5? Nej. Bara med 11.
steps.title: ûr x + y ocksûË alltid delbart med 2, 3, 5?

steps.text: Svaret ûÊr D ã 11. Insikten i en mening: nûÊr du kastar om siffror i ett tvûËsiffrigt tal och adderar, ûÊr resultatet ALLTID 11ôñ(siffersumma) ã eftersom 10 + 1 = 11.
steps.title: Slutsats

var-2019-kvant2-XYZ-010.technique: Siffer-omkastnings-strategi: fûÑr tvûËsiffriga tal x = 10a + b och y = 10b + a, ûÊr x + y = 11(a + b) och x ã y = 9(a ã b). Triggern: 'siffror i omvûÊnd ordning' ã faktorisera 10ôñs + s = 11ôñs, eller 10ôñs ã s = 9ôñs.

pregrade_tactic.move: TvûËsiffrigt tal (a, b) ûÊr 10a + b; omvûÊnd ordning ûÊr 10b + a ã summan blir 11(a + b), differensen 9(a ã b).

var-2019-kvant2-XYZ-011.solution_path: Faktorisera vûÊnsterledet: Ÿx^3+x^2-2x=x(x^2+x-2)=x(x+2)(x-1)Ÿ. NollstûÊllena ûÊr Ÿx=0Ÿ, Ÿx=-2Ÿ och Ÿx=1Ÿ. VûÊrdet Ÿx=-1Ÿ ûÊr INTE en lûÑsning ã alternativ C.

steps.title: FûÑrstûË problemet
steps.text: Du har tredjegradsekvationen Ÿx^3+x^2-2x=0Ÿ och fyra alternativ. FrûËgan: vilket alternativ ûÊr INTE en lûÑsning? (Ovanligt ã leta efter felaktigt vûÊrde.)

steps.title: Vad betyder lûÑsning?
steps.text: Definition: ett tal ûÊr lûÑsning till en ekvation om det gûÑr VL = HL. HûÊr ûÊr HL = 0, sûË ett x-vûÊrde ûÊr lûÑsning omm uttrycket VL blir noll nûÊr vi sûÊtter in det.

steps.title: Bryt ut gemensam faktor
steps.text: Alla tre termer (Ÿx^3Ÿ, Ÿx^2Ÿ, Ÿ-2xŸ) innehûËller faktorn ŸxŸ. Bryt ut den: Ÿx^3+x^2-2x=x(x^2+x-2)Ÿ. Det ûÊr distributiva lagen baklûÊnges.

steps.title: Nollproduktregeln
steps.text: Regel: om en produkt ûÊr 0 mûËste minst en faktor vara 0. SûË Ÿx(x^2+x-2)=0Ÿ betyder antingen Ÿx=0Ÿ ELLER Ÿx^2+x-2=0Ÿ.

steps.title: FûÑrsta lûÑsning klar
steps.text: Direkt frûËn faktorn ŸxŸ: en lûÑsning ûÊr Ÿx=0Ÿ. Det matchar alternativ A ã A ûÊr alltsûË en lûÑsning.

steps.title: Faktorisera kvadrattermen
steps.text: FûÑr Ÿx^2+x-2Ÿ sûÑk tvûË tal vars produkt ûÊr Ÿ-2Ÿ och summa ûÊr Ÿ+1Ÿ. Talen Ÿ+2Ÿ och Ÿ-1Ÿ fungerar: Ÿ+2\cdot(-1)=-2Ÿ och Ÿ+2+(-1)=+1Ÿ. AlltsûË Ÿx^2+x-2=(x+2)(x-1)Ÿ.

steps.title: Tre lûÑsningar totalt
steps.text: Hela faktoriseringen: Ÿx(x+2)(x-1)=0Ÿ. Nollproduktregeln ger lûÑsningarna Ÿx=0Ÿ, Ÿx+2=0\Rightarrow x=-2Ÿ, och Ÿx-1=0\Rightarrow x=1Ÿ.

steps.title: JûÊmfûÑr med alternativen
steps.text: Alternativen ûÊr A: 0, B: 1, C: ã1, D: ã2. LûÑsningarna ûÊr {0, 1, ã2}. VûÊrdet Ÿ-1Ÿ finns INTE i lûÑsningsmûÊngden.

steps.title: KontrollrûÊkning fûÑr C
steps.text: PrûÑva Ÿx=-1Ÿ: Ÿ(-1)^3+(-1)^2-2(-1)=-1+1+2=2\neq 0Ÿ. InsûÊttningen ger 2, inte 0, sûË x = ã1 ûÊr inte lûÑsning. BekrûÊftar steg 8.

steps.title: Slutsats
steps.text: Svaret ûÊr C. Insikten i en mening: faktorisera fûÑrst ã dûË syns alla lûÑsningar direkt, och du slipper testa varje alternativ blint.

distractors.why_tempting: Det ûÊr lûÊtt att tro att 0 inte 'fûËr' vara lûÑsning eftersom den kûÊnns trivial.
distractors.why_wrong: Steg 5 visar att x = 0 ûÊr en av tre lûÑsningar ã faktorn ŸxŸ ger den direkt.

distractors.why_tempting: Om du minns regeln som 'koefficient + konstant' kan du tro att 1 inte fungerar.
distractors.why_wrong: Steg 7 och 10 visar att x = 1 sûÊtter den andra faktorn Ÿ(x-1)Ÿ till 0 ã alltsûË ûÊr 1 lûÑsning.

distractors.why_tempting: MûËnga stannar vid ã2 och tror att det negativa vûÊrdet ûÊr 'fel-svaret'.
distractors.why_wrong: Steg 7 visar att x = ã2 nollstûÊller faktorn Ÿ(x+2)Ÿ ã alltsûË ûÊr ã2 ocksûË lûÑsning.

var-2019-kvant2-XYZ-011.technique: Faktorisera-fûÑr-att-finna-alla-rûÑtter: bryt ut gemensam faktor fûÑrst, faktorisera resten, lûÊs av nollstûÊllena. Trigger: tredjegradsekvation utan konstantterm ã du vet att en faktor ûÊr x.
var-2019-kvant2-XYZ-011.pitfall: Botemedlet: nûÊr frûËgan ûÊr 'vilket ûÊr INTE lûÑsning?' ã rûÊkna ut hela lûÑsningsmûÊngden fûÑrst, och jûÊmfûÑr sedan. Annars ûÊr det lûÊtt att rûËka stryka en korrekt lûÑsning som 'fel'.

pregrade_tactic.move: Om polynomet saknar konstantterm: bryt ut x. Faktorisera resten med produkt/summa-tricket. LûÊs av alla rûÑtter ur faktorerna.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'researcher Luigi Ferrucci' and the confident tone of the findings and infer that researchers have now solved the age-personality question.
distractors.why_wrong: The paragraph claims the opposite ã personality 'doesn't change much after age 30', so there is no rich 'how age shapes our personality' story to understand. Step 3 makes this explicit.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at the closing line about dementia and read it as a claim that age-related diseases are rising.
distractors.why_wrong: Dementia is mentioned only as an alternative explanation for personality change, not as a trend. Step 2 quotes the line ã 'could be a sign of disease' ã without any frequency claim.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct is to latch onto the number 30 and assume the study found two distinct groups (under-30 vs over-30).
distractors.why_wrong: The number 30 marks STABILITY ('doesn't change much after age 30'), not a split into two patterns. Step 5 shows D inverts the finding.

var-2019-verb1-ELF-031.solution_path: The 'Aging' paragraph reports that an old assumption ã people get grumpier with age ã was overturned by Baltimore longitudinal data showing personality is stable after 30. Grumpiness reflects youth-era hardship, not aging itself. That matches A: previous claims about age-driven personality change were overstated.

steps.text: The prompt is 'What is the main point here?' ã you need the single idea the whole 'Aging' paragraph is built around, not a side detail. Main-point items reward the headline, not the example. Read the paragraph once, then ask: if I had to title it in one sentence, what would I say?
steps.title: Understand the question

steps.text: Two sentences carry the argument. Ferrucci's finding: 'an adult's personality generally doesn't change much after age 30.' And the reframe of 'grumpy old men': they are 'gruff not because of age, but because they're set in a personality shaped by their youth during times when war, poverty and other harsh experiences were more common.'
steps.title: Locate the key sentences

steps.text: Boiled down: we used to think aging makes people grumpy. New data says no ã personality barely changes after 30, and grumpy older people were probably already grumpy because of a hard youth. The previous belief was an overclaim.
steps.title: Restate in plain English

steps.text: 'Overstated' = exaggerated, claimed more strongly than the evidence supports. 'Longitudinal' = following the same people over many years. 'Set in a personality' = locked into a stable temperament. 'Dementia' = a brain-disease cluster that can change personality, mentioned as a separate cause.
steps.title: Vocabulary check

steps.text: Walk each option. A ('Age-related changes in personality have previously been overstated') maps directly onto the reframe ã the old grumpy-old-men idea was wrong; aging itself doesn't drive change. B says researchers 'finally' understand how age shapes personality, but the paragraph says age does NOT shape personality much after 30. C invents 'age-related diseases on the increase' ã the text only mentions disease as a possible cause of change, not as an epidemic. D says researchers found 'separate personality patterns' under and over 30, but the finding is the opposite ã patterns are continuous, not split.
steps.title: Match against the options

steps.text: The answer is A. The paragraph's headline is that an earlier belief about aging and personality was overturned ã i.e., previously overstated. The 'grumpy old men' example is the illustration; A names the headline.
steps.title: Conclusion

var-2019-verb1-ELF-031.technique: Main-point ELF: find the sentence that names a REFRAMING or REVERSAL ã 'we used to believe X, butãÎ' ã that sentence almost always equals the correct option. Trigger to memorise: when a passage opens with an old belief and pivots to new data, the main point is the overturning.

pregrade_tactic.move: When the text opens with 'we used to believe X, but...', the main point is the revaluation itself ã pick the option that names the new view.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'moving their gallows behind grey prison walls' as 'ending executions' ã the prose has the feel of an ending.
distractors.why_wrong: Moving hangings indoors is not the same as abolishing them. Step 3 makes the distinction: the spectacle moved private, the penalty stayed. A imports an event the paragraph never describes.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at the rowdy 'Victorian mob' image and infer a familiar story ã the public eventually moved on to other entertainments.
distractors.why_wrong: The paragraph explicitly denies this with the line 'it wasn't due to dwindling public interest.' Step 2 quotes the denial; B picks the option the text rules out.

distractors.why_tempting: If you treat Dickens's name as a sign that the upper class engaged with the spectacle, D feels close ã the upper class participated.
distractors.why_wrong: Dickens is named as a CAMPAIGNER who found the crowds 'uncivilised and cruel' ã the opposite of someone who took pleasure in watching. Step 5 separates engagement from enjoyment.

var-2019-verb1-ELF-032.solution_path: The 'Executions' paragraph notes that nineteenth-century Britain moved hangings indoors and that 'why this decision was taken is hotly debated.' That direct hedge ã 'hotly debated' ã matches C: there is no consensus about why public hangings ended.

steps.text: The prompt is 'What is said here concerning executions in Britain?' ã you need the option that is actually CLAIMED by the paragraph, not the option that sounds historically plausible. Detail-question discipline: a match has to come from the text, not from background knowledge.
steps.title: Understand the question

steps.text: Two anchors. On the timing: 'In the 19th centuryãÎ many western nations began moving their gallows behind grey prison walls.' On the cause: 'Why this decision was taken is hotly debated, but it wasn't due to dwindling public interest.' Plus the 1868 Newgate detail showing crowds of 2,000 still showed up.
steps.title: Locate the key sentences

steps.text: In short: 19th-century Britain stopped doing executions in public and moved them inside prisons. Historians don't agree on why. We know the cause was NOT that the public got bored ã crowds were still huge and rowdy. Dickens-style protests against the spectacle may have helped.
steps.title: Restate in plain English

steps.text: 'Hotly debated' = strongly disputed; no agreement among scholars. 'Consensus' = general agreement. 'Dwindling' = shrinking, getting smaller. 'Furore' = uproar, a noisy public outcry. The phrase 'hotly debated' and the word 'consensus' track each other: hotly debated means there is no consensus.
steps.title: Vocabulary check

steps.text: Walk each option. A says the death penalty was abolished in the mid-19th century, but the paragraph is about moving hangings indoors, not abolishing them ã A confuses 'made private' with 'ended'. B says executions stopped when Victorians found other amusement, but the text explicitly denies that ('it wasn't due to dwindling public interest'). C says there's no consensus about why public hangings ended ã directly matches 'hotly debated'. D says even upper-class people enjoyed watching, but the paragraph mentions Dickens as a campaigner AGAINST the spectacle, not as an enjoyer.
steps.title: Match against the options

steps.text: The answer is C. 'Hotly debated' is the text's own way of saying 'historians don't agree' ã that is exactly what 'no consensus' means in option C.
steps.title: Conclusion

var-2019-verb1-ELF-032.technique: Detail ELF: when the paragraph uses a hedge like 'hotly debated', 'still unclear', 'a mystery', or 'no agreement', expect the correct option to PARAPHRASE that hedge. Trigger: a phrase signalling uncertainty maps onto an option with words like 'no consensus', 'not yet established', 'still unclear'.

pregrade_tactic.move: When the text uses hedges like 'hotly debated', 'still unclear', 'no consensus', hunt for options that preserve the same uncertainty.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'coins vs notes' as a claim about how much different people give ã variation across donors.
distractors.why_wrong: The paragraph isn't comparing different donors; it's comparing the SAME behaviour under different visibility conditions. Step 3 makes the mechanism explicit ã same population, different method, different giving.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at the surface detail ã two churches, two methods ã and pick the option that summarises the visible facts.
distractors.why_wrong: B describes the SETUP of the anecdote, not its POINT. The author isn't writing about church accounting practices; he's writing about what those practices reveal about donors. Step 5 separates setup from argument.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct is that 'social factors' covers everything about visibility and donation, so D feels safely correct.
distractors.why_wrong: D is true in a too-broad way ã it would fit almost any sociology essay. The opening paragraph names the SPECIFIC social factor (being watched), and C captures that specificity. Step 5 flags 'vague-true' as the trap.

var-2019-verb1-ELF-033.solution_path: The opening paragraph of 'Doing Good' contrasts a bag (coins, hidden) with a tray (notes, visible) and concludes the difference is in how much people give ã because being seen changes behaviour. That matches C: people give more when they know they are being watched.

steps.text: The prompt asks for the MAIN ARGUMENT in the opening paragraph ã the one claim the bag-and-tray anecdote is being told to make. With main-argument questions, the anecdote is the evidence; you need the headline, not the detail.
steps.title: Understand the question

steps.text: Two sentences carry the argument. The set-up: 'One took its weekly collection by passing around a cloth bag, while at the other the congregation placed their offerings on a tray.' The punchline: 'People would put coins into a bag because others could only hear and not see that they had donated, whereas they would be more inclined to put notes onto a tray as a public display of their generosity.'
steps.title: Locate the key sentences

steps.text: Boiled down: when a donation is hidden (in a bag), people give less and give coins. When it's visible (on a tray), people give more and give notes. The difference isn't the church or the cause ã it's whether other people can see what you gave. Being watched makes donors more generous.
steps.title: Restate in plain English

steps.text: 'Conspicuous' = clearly visible, noticeable to others. 'Endemic cynicism' = the widely held suspicion that selfless acts are really selfish. 'Public display' = something done so others can see. The opening paragraph keys on visibility ã note 'see' vs 'hear', 'public display'.
steps.title: Vocabulary check

steps.text: Walk each option. A says people differ in how much they donate ã true broadly, but the paragraph isn't about person-to-person variation; it's about HOW THE COLLECTION METHOD changes the SAME people's giving. B says churches favour different methods ã descriptively true but trivial; not the argument the bag-vs-tray contrast is making. C says people give more if they know they're being watched ã this is exactly the punchline. D says 'social factors are relevant' ã TRUE in a vague sense, but vague-true is the classic overreach trap; C is the SPECIFIC social factor the paragraph names.
steps.title: Match against the options

steps.text: The answer is C. The opening anecdote is told to make one point: visibility changes giving. D names the right category (social factors) but loses the specific mechanism; C nails it.
steps.title: Conclusion

var-2019-verb1-ELF-033.technique: Main-argument ELF: an anecdote in paragraph one is always told to make a single point. Distil the anecdote into 'X causes Y' and pick the option that names that causal claim. Trigger: when an option restates the SETUP rather than the CONCLUSION, it's a distractor; when an option names the right CATEGORY but loses the specific mechanism, it's also a distractor.

pregrade_tactic.move: An opening anecdote is always there to illustrate a thesis ã distill the story to 'X causes Y' and pick the option that names that conclusion.

distractors.why_tempting: If you read 'comically naû₤ve' as strong, B feels like the natural escalation: not just doubted, but actually proved false.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage describes a SOCIAL ATTITUDE, not a scientific verdict. Step 3 marks the distinction: people TREAT it as illusion, but the text doesn't claim it's been proved one.

distractors.why_tempting: The Catholic-church opening makes religious framing salient; it's tempting to fold goodness of heart into 'religious dogma'.
distractors.why_wrong: The church anecdote is about visibility and donation, not about religious belief in goodness. Step 2 quotes the relevant lines ã none mentions dogma. The religious setting is scenery, not argument.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at the elegiac tone of the passage and infer that goodness itself is in decline.
distractors.why_wrong: The paragraph is about declining BELIEF in goodness, not declining goodness. People may still be kind; the passage says we just don't trust their kindness anymore. Step 5 separates attitudes from rates.

var-2019-verb1-ELF-034.solution_path: The passage frames pure 'goodness of heart' as something modern readers no longer take at face value ã 'You are seen as comically naû₤ve if you believeãÎ' and 'this reaction is no longer seen as cynical at all, but simply realistic.' That matches A: goodness of heart is met with widespread scepticism today.

steps.text: The prompt is 'What are we told about goodness of heart as a human property?' ã you need the claim the passage actually makes about how people now view goodness of heart, not a general moral statement about human nature.
steps.title: Understand the question

steps.text: Three lines carry the claim. 'You are seen as comically naû₤ve if you believe that people give up their time or money purely out of the goodness of their own hearts.' Then: '"What's in it for them?" is often the automatic response to signs of conspicuous altruism.' And the kicker: 'Indeed, this reaction is no longer seen as cynical at all, but simply realistic.'
steps.title: Locate the key sentences

steps.text: Boiled down: today, if you say someone is genuinely good-hearted, people roll their eyes. The default response is to look for the hidden selfish motive. That default suspicion has become mainstream ã not cynical, just 'realistic' according to current attitudes.
steps.title: Restate in plain English

steps.text: 'Scepticism' = doubt, unwillingness to believe a claim without strong evidence. 'Endemic cynicism' = pervasive suspicion of motives. 'Comically naû₤ve' = laughably innocent. 'Conspicuous altruism' = visible generosity. Notice the cluster: naû₤ve, cynicism, suspicion ã they all signal the same modern doubt.
steps.title: Vocabulary check

steps.text: Walk each option. A says goodness of heart is viewed sceptically nowadays ã exactly matches 'seen as comically naû₤ve' and 'no longer cynical, just realistic'. B says it's been 'proved an illusion' ã the passage says people TREAT it as illusion, but it doesn't claim science has proved it. C says it's 'wishful thinking dressed up as religious dogma' ã the passage uses church examples but never reduces goodness to religious dogma. D says it's a 'noble instinct becoming rare' ã the passage isn't about decline in actual kindness; it's about decline in BELIEF in kindness.
steps.title: Match against the options

steps.text: The answer is A. The passage's claim is about modern ATTITUDES toward goodness of heart, not about whether goodness has actually declined. A names the attitude ã scepticism ã precisely.
steps.title: Conclusion

var-2019-verb1-ELF-034.technique: Attitude ELF: when the passage uses phrases like 'seen as', 'viewed as', 'no longer X but Y', the question is about PERCEPTION, not REALITY. Trigger: pick the option that describes the perception explicitly ('met with scepticism', 'widely doubted'), not the option that ESCALATES the perception into a proven fact.

pregrade_tactic.move: On expressions like 'seen as', 'viewed as', 'no longer X but Y', the question is about perception ã pick options that describe the perception, not the reality.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read the passage's worry about 'eco-narcissism' and 'doing good primarily to make yourself feel better' as a critique of moral-ego boosting.
distractors.why_wrong: Baggini's worry about eco-narcissism is a SIDE CAUTION, not his main critique. His central position is that benefitting yourself while giving is fine. Step 3 anchors the main target ã purity demands, not ego boosts.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at the 'clear conscience' theme and pair it with wealth ã buying a guilt-free life sounds plausible.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage never frames the problem in terms of wealth or who can afford conscience. Step 2's anchors show the target is purity, not affordability.

distractors.why_tempting: Snap reading might pick up 'political' from the broader sense of donations and ethics being political acts.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage doesn't discuss political acceptability at all. C imports a category that isn't in the text. Step 5 confirms there is no anchor for it.

var-2019-verb1-ELF-035.solution_path: Baggini argues against the demand that 'genuine altruism is never tainted by self-interest' ã he calls this 'puritanism' and asks 'why should we ever think that in the first place?' That makes D the problem: the argument that only completely unselfish donations count as truly ethical.

steps.text: The prompt asks what is ESPECIALLY PROBLEMATIC about discussions of charity, according to the writer. So you need to find the specific stance the writer flags as wrong-headed, not just any criticism of charity in the passage.
steps.title: Understand the question

steps.text: The criticism is concentrated in two passages. First: 'Cynicism about charity becomes inevitable once you demand that genuine altruism is never tainted by self-interest. But why should we ever think that in the first place?' Then: 'There's a kind of puritanism in the idea that charity must hurt to really count.'
steps.title: Locate the key sentences

steps.text: Boiled down: Baggini's target is the demand that real charity must be PURE ã no benefit to the giver, no self-interest involved. He calls this stance puritanical and says it's the SOURCE of the cynicism that poisons how we talk about charity. The problem is the all-or-nothing definition of ethical giving.
steps.title: Restate in plain English

steps.text: 'Puritanism' = a strict, austere outlook that treats pleasure or self-interest as suspect. 'Tainted' = contaminated, made impure. 'Selfless' = without thought for oneself. 'Begrudgingly' = unwillingly, with reluctance. The reluctant-donor example is the clincher: the puritan view forces us to call the unwilling giver more virtuous than the joyful one.
steps.title: Vocabulary check

steps.text: Walk each option. A says boosting one's moral ego via giving is the problem ã but Baggini DEFENDS this; he says feeling good about giving is fine. B says only wealthy people can buy a clear conscience ã the passage doesn't focus on wealth at all. C says giving money to those in need is politically unacceptable ã never said in the text. D says the problem is the argument that only completely UNSELFISH donations are truly ethical ã that's exactly the puritan stance Baggini attacks.
steps.title: Match against the options

steps.text: The answer is D. Baggini's whole second half is built against the purity test for charity. He thinks demanding pure motives is the thing that makes charitable discourse toxic ã and D names that demand precisely.
steps.title: Conclusion

var-2019-verb1-ELF-035.technique: Author's-target ELF: when the prompt asks what the writer finds problematic, locate the stance the author argues AGAINST ã usually marked by 'puritanism', 'illusion', 'the demand thatãÎ', or rhetorical questions like 'why should we ever think that?'. Trigger: an option that names the AUTHOR'S OPPONENT's view is correct; an option that names a side-caution or a true-but-tangential point is a distractor.

pregrade_tactic.move: On 'what does the writer find problematic' items, hunt for the position the writer argues AGAINST ã marked by 'illusion', 'puritanism', rhetorical questions.

distractors.why_tempting: The 'eco-narcissism' passage is vivid and easy to remember; A makes that vividness the headline.
distractors.why_wrong: Eco-narcissism is a CAUTION, not Baggini's main view. He spends most of the essay defending mixed motives and ends on the win-win line. Step 3 separates side cautions from main claim.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at the Mother Teresa critique and infer Baggini is broadly sceptical of charity's track record.
distractors.why_wrong: The Mother Teresa passage is an example of the CULTURE of suspicion, not Baggini's own audit. Baggini ends with charity as a 'win-win deal' ã the opposite of severe limitations. Step 2 anchors the final stance.

distractors.why_tempting: If you scan for 'risks' to recipients, the 'harms the recipient' line in the text feels close to dependency.
distractors.why_wrong: The text mentions HARM, not dependency. Long-term dependency is an idea D imports; Baggini's worry is narrower ã that 'apparent altruism helps only the giver'. Step 5 catches the import.

var-2019-verb1-ELF-036.solution_path: Baggini's bottom line: 'Nor should we worry too much about motivationãÎ just as long as what they do genuinely helps others, too' and 'The purpose of charity is to make life easier for everyone.' He cares about EFFECTS, not motives ã that matches B: what counts is the effects, not the causes of the will to help.

steps.text: The prompt asks for the AUTHOR'S MAIN VIEW of charity ã Baggini's overall stance, distilled. A summary question rewards the option that captures the entire argument's payoff, not a side observation.
steps.title: Understand the question

steps.text: Three closing lines carry the view. 'People's motives are invariably mixed, and if self-interest is among them, we shouldn't worry, just as long as what they do genuinely helps others, too.' Then: 'Problems only arise if apparent altruism helps only the giver and perhaps even harms the recipient.' And: 'The purpose of charity is to make life easier for everyone.'
steps.title: Locate the key sentences

steps.text: Boiled down: motives are messy, mostly mixed, and that's OK. What we should care about is whether the help actually helps. If a giver also benefits, fine ã even good. The whole game is about consequences for the people involved, not the purity of the giver's heart.
steps.title: Restate in plain English

steps.text: 'Benefactor' = the giver, the one helping. 'Recipient' = the one receiving help. 'Self-interest' = caring about your own benefit. 'Hypocrisy' = saying one thing and doing another. 'Win-win' = both parties gain ã Baggini's closing image. Notice the cluster: effects, helps, benefits, recipient ã the whole vocabulary is about RESULTS, not MOTIVES.
steps.title: Vocabulary check

steps.text: Walk each option. A says some of charity's psychological consequences are questionable ã Baggini does mention eco-narcissism, but that's a side caution, not his main view; A makes the side note the headline. B says what counts is the EFFECTS, not the CAUSES of the will to help ã exactly Baggini's position. C says charity's results so far show practical limitations ã the passage doesn't audit charity's track record at all. D says recipients risk becoming long-term dependent ã never mentioned; the recipient-side worry the passage names is 'harm', not dependency.
steps.title: Match against the options

steps.text: The answer is B. Baggini's whole essay drives toward a consequence-based ethics of charity: stop policing motives, judge by outcomes. B distils that into one sentence.
steps.title: Conclusion

var-2019-verb1-ELF-036.technique: Author's-view-summary ELF: when the prompt asks for the MAIN VIEW, the answer almost always sits in the closing paragraph's payoff sentence. Trigger: locate the sentence that names the author's POSITIVE position (what they DO endorse, not what they criticise) and pick the option that paraphrases it.

pregrade_tactic.move: On 'main view' or 'main argument' items, the answer usually sits in the final paragraph's pay-off sentence ã the positive position the writer actually defends.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'eat locally grown organic' as a form of self-denial ã you're skipping cheaper or tastier options.
distractors.why_wrong: Self-denial describes the BEHAVIOUR of ethical consumerism, not the RISK Baggini names. The risk is feeling smug ABOUT the denial. Step 3 keeps the focus on the named risk.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at the protective tone ('keep ourselves pure') and pick B because 'pure' sounds defensive.
distractors.why_wrong: 'Keep ourselves pure' means staying morally clean in our own eyes, not protecting ourselves from danger. Self-protection would name an external threat; the threat here is internal smugness. Step 4 separates the moods.

distractors.why_tempting: If you read 'concern for the environment' as image-conscious behaviour, self-consciousness sounds close ã awareness of how you're seen.
distractors.why_wrong: Self-consciousness is OUTWARD-FACING and uncomfortable; the eco-narcissism Baggini describes is INWARD-FACING and comfortable. He uses words like 'feel good', 'pure' ã the language of contentment with oneself. Step 5 maps the mismatch.

var-2019-verb1-ELF-037.solution_path: Baggini's worry about ethical consumerism is 'eco-narcissism, where the main priority is to keep ourselves pure' ã choosing organic food, low-energy bulbs, and non-GM produce to feel good about ourselves. The single word that captures that risk is C: self-satisfaction.

steps.text: The prompt asks which word best reflects the SPECIFIC risk discussed about ethical consumerism. So you need to match a labelled mood ã pride, smugness, defensiveness ã to the exact danger Baggini names, not to ethical consumerism in general.
steps.title: Understand the question

steps.text: Two sentences carry the risk. 'Concern for the environment can easily become a form of eco-narcissism, where the main priority is to keep ourselves pure.' Then the rhetorical questions: 'But how altruistic is it to eat locally grown organic produce while refusing to spend money on food from countries that need our trade? How entitled are you to feel good about fitting a few low-energy light bulbs if you fly even once a year?'
steps.title: Locate the key sentences

steps.text: Boiled down: the danger of ethical consumerism is that it becomes about FEELING GOOD ABOUT YOURSELF rather than about doing good. You feel virtuous because you bought the organic apple, even though the bigger picture (poor countries' trade, the flight you took) tells against you. The mood at the heart of this is smugness ã being pleased with yourself.
steps.title: Restate in plain English

steps.text: 'Self-denial' = refusing yourself pleasures or comforts. 'Self-protection' = guarding yourself from harm. 'Self-satisfaction' = being pleased with yourself, often with a hint of smugness. 'Self-consciousness' = awkward awareness of being observed. The phrase 'feel good about' (in the rhetorical question) and 'keep ourselves pure' are pure self-satisfaction territory ã both describe the inward pleasure of feeling virtuous.
steps.title: Vocabulary check

steps.text: Walk each option. A 'self-denial' is the OPPOSITE ã ethical consumerism IS a form of self-denial (skip the GM food, skip the cheap import), but the RISK Baggini names isn't 'you'll deny yourself too much'; it's 'you'll feel good about yourself wrongly'. B 'self-protection' doesn't fit ã Baggini doesn't suggest these consumers are protecting themselves from anything. C 'self-satisfaction' captures 'feel good about', 'keep ourselves pure' ã the smug, virtuous-feeling state. D 'self-consciousness' would mean being awkwardly aware of being watched; the risk is the OPPOSITE ã too comfortable in one's own moral image.
steps.title: Match against the options

steps.text: The answer is C. 'Self-satisfaction' is the single word that names the inward smugness Baggini diagnoses ã feeling good about yourself for small acts that may not even be the most helpful. The other 'self-' words name different moods that don't match the description.
steps.title: Conclusion

var-2019-verb1-ELF-037.technique: Single-word match ELF: when the question hands you four 'self-' compounds, decode each independently in plain English, then pick the one whose definition overlaps the paragraph's most LOADED phrase. Trigger: phrases like 'feel good about', 'keep ourselves pure', 'eco-narcissism' map onto self-satisfaction; phrases like 'avoid', 'protect from' map onto self-protection ã match the mood, not the topic.

pregrade_tactic.move: On single-word items, restate each option in plain prose and match against the text's most loaded phrase ('feel good about' ã self-satisfaction).

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read the optimistic opening ã 'one of the happiest trends' ã and infer the MDGs helped, so without them the decline would have been smaller.
distractors.why_wrong: The paragraph explicitly says we can't know that ã it's 'the main difficultyãÎ to guess what would have happened had the MDGs not been set.' Step 2 quotes the difficulty; A picks the verdict the text refuses.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at the date pairing (MDGs set in 2000, decline observed in the 21st century) and infer causation: the MDGs kicked it off.
distractors.why_wrong: The text directly denies this: 'during the 1990s, the number of child deaths was already falling steeply.' The trend pre-dates the MDGs. Step 5 catches the timeline.

distractors.why_tempting: If you read the 1990s pre-trend as showing the MDGs didn't add much, D feels like the natural inference.
distractors.why_wrong: Same trap as A in mirror image ã D picks a specific verdict (minor role) that the paragraph deliberately leaves open. Step 3's stance is 'unsettled', not 'minor'.

var-2019-verb1-ELF-038.solution_path: The 'Infant Mortality' paragraph says working out the MDGs' contribution 'has become a big part of the debate' and notes the difficulty: 'to guess what would have happened had the MDGs not been set; during the 1990s, the number of child deaths was already falling steeply.' That matches B: the MDGs' effect has not yet been clearly established.

steps.text: The prompt asks what is said about the decline in infant mortality ã specifically about HOW MUCH of that decline can be credited to the Millennium Development Goals. So you need the option that captures the text's stance on attribution.
steps.title: Understand the question

steps.text: Two anchors. The framing: 'Working out the extent to which the MDGs were responsible for this decline has become a big part of the debate over whether they were a success or not.' The problem: 'The main difficulty is to guess what would have happened had the MDGs not been set; during the 1990s, the number of child deaths was already falling steeply.'
steps.title: Locate the key sentences

steps.text: Boiled down: infant mortality has dropped a lot. But how much credit goes to the MDGs is unsettled. The decline was already happening in the 1990s before the MDGs existed, so it's hard to tell what the MDGs actually added. The contribution is debated, not established.
steps.title: Restate in plain English

steps.text: 'Established' = settled, clearly demonstrated. 'Pronounced' = strongly marked, visible. 'Counterfactual' = what would have happened in a different world (the 'had the MDGs not been set' move). 'Initiated' = started. The paragraph uses the language of UNCERTAINTY ã 'debate', 'difficulty', 'to guess' ã and that maps directly onto 'has not yet been clearly established'.
steps.title: Vocabulary check

steps.text: Walk each option. A says the decline would have been less pronounced without the MDGs ã that's a SPECIFIC claim of MDG impact, but the paragraph says we can't actually tell. B says the effect has not yet been clearly established ã directly matches 'debate' and 'difficulty to guess'. C says the MDGs initiated the trend ã but the text explicitly says the decline was already happening in the 1990s, before the MDGs (year 2000). D says only a minor role for the MDGs ã again a specific verdict the paragraph refuses to give.
steps.title: Match against the options

steps.text: The answer is B. The paragraph's whole stance is 'we don't know yet'. B is the only option that preserves that uncertainty; A, C, and D each pick a side the text deliberately doesn't pick.
steps.title: Conclusion

var-2019-verb1-ELF-038.technique: Attribution-uncertainty ELF: when a paragraph describes a trend and then says the cause is 'debated' or 'hard to establish', the correct option preserves the uncertainty ã usually phrased as 'has not yet been clearly established' or 'remains unclear'. Trigger: any option that gives a SPECIFIC verdict (definitely caused it, definitely didn't) is wrong; the text is refusing to pick a side.

pregrade_tactic.move: On 'debated' or 'hard to establish' items about a cause, preserve the uncertainty in your option ã rule out anything that delivers a settled verdict.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'orange patches' and 'attractive to mates' together and conclude the orange colour is the mating signal.
distractors.why_wrong: The mating signal is specifically the ULTRAVIOLET layer, not the visible orange. Step 3 separates the two layers ã only one is described as varying with maturity and pairing.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'striking color patches' and 'penguins are able to see' and infer king penguins are exceptionally varied compared to other birds.
distractors.why_wrong: The paragraph never compares king penguins to other species in terms of colour variation. C imports a comparison that isn't there. Step 5 confirms no anchor.

distractors.why_tempting: If you read 'higher ultraviolet reflectance in recently formed pairs' too quickly, you can flip it and infer the colour fades AFTER pairing.
distractors.why_wrong: The text says UV reflectance is HIGHER in newly paired birds ã i.e. brighter, not faded. D reverses the direction. Step 2 quotes the line; the increase is in the pair-formed group.

var-2019-verb1-ELF-039.solution_path: King penguins have orange beak patches visible to humans, plus ULTRAVIOLET patches 'imperceptible to human eyes' that penguins can see. That matches B: they use colour markings that cannot be perceived by humans.

steps.text: The prompt is 'What is said about king penguins?' ã a broad detail question. You need an option that the paragraph DIRECTLY claims, not one that's plausibly true about penguins from general knowledge.
steps.title: Understand the question

steps.text: Two anchor lines. The visible part: 'In the visible light spectrum, these beak patches appear orange.' The hidden part: 'Penguins are able to see into the ultraviolet region of the electromagnetic spectrum, however, and can detect additional patches of ultraviolet color along the lower bill that are imperceptible to human eyes.'
steps.title: Locate the key sentences

steps.text: Boiled down: king penguins have two layers of beak colouring. One layer is orange and we can see it. The other layer is ultraviolet ã we CAN'T see it, but penguins can. The ultraviolet patches seem to signal sexual maturity and may help with mating.
steps.title: Restate in plain English

steps.text: 'Ultraviolet' = light with wavelengths shorter than visible violet; invisible to humans but visible to many animals. 'Imperceptible' = unable to be perceived, not detectable. 'Hue' = a shade or tone of colour. 'Reflectance' = how much light a surface bounces back. The whole point is the gap between what humans see (orange) and what penguins see (orange + UV).
steps.title: Vocabulary check

steps.text: Walk each option. A says orange is thought to function as a mating signal ã but the paragraph says the ULTRAVIOLET hue rises with maturity and shows up in newly paired birds; the mating-signal claim attaches to UV, not to orange. B says they use colour markings humans cannot perceive ã directly matches the UV-patches-imperceptible-to-humans line. C says greater within-species colour variation than other species ã never claimed; the paragraph doesn't compare to other species. D says orange patches fade after pairing ã the text says UV reflectance is HIGHER in paired birds, not lower; D inverts the direction.
steps.title: Match against the options

steps.text: The answer is B. The paragraph's key claim is the human-perception gap: there are markings on the beak that we cannot see. B captures that gap exactly.
steps.title: Conclusion

var-2019-verb1-ELF-039.technique: Two-layer detail ELF: when a paragraph describes a single object with TWO PROPERTIES (here: visible orange vs invisible ultraviolet), distractors typically attach one property's behaviour to the other. Trigger: when an option mentions 'orange', re-check whether the relevant claim was actually made about orange or about ultraviolet ã most wrong options swap the layers.

pregrade_tactic.move: When the text describes an object with two properties (orange vs ultraviolet), check that the option links the right property to the right claim.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'Why we do this is still a mystery' and 'Also mysterious is whyãÎ' and infer that the whole act of crying is unexplained.
distractors.why_wrong: The mysteries in the paragraph are narrow ã moral crying and adult tears-of-joy. The early-life mechanics (babies bawling for caregivers, adolescents crying from emotional pain) are clearly explained. Step 3 maps which parts are explained and which are open.

distractors.why_tempting: The paragraph mentions 'tears of joy' explicitly, so an option naming joy-vs-sadness origins feels close.
distractors.why_wrong: The text actually says tears of joy may NOT reflect happiness ã they're tied to bittersweet awareness of mortality. So joy-tears and sadness-tears share an origin in the paragraph's theory, not differ. B inverts the claim. Step 2 quotes the bittersweet line.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at the variety of crying types and infer that culture must be the driver ã different societies, different crying norms.
distractors.why_wrong: The paragraph never mentions culture. Its structuring variable is AGE, not society. Step 5 catches the import ã D names a category the text doesn't use.

var-2019-verb1-ELF-040.solution_path: The 'Crying' paragraph traces how crying CHANGES across life stages: babies cry from physical needs, adolescents cry more from emotional pain, then 'moral crying' and tears-of-joy emerge as we age. The pattern is age-linked and described as following a course. That matches C: characteristics of crying shift in predictable ways throughout life.

steps.text: The prompt asks what is CLAIMED about the act of crying. So you need the option the paragraph actually asserts, not a plausible-sounding general truth about why we cry. Match the paragraph's structural claim, not its examples.
steps.title: Understand the question

steps.text: Three lines build the trajectory. 'Human babies excel at the former [wailing]ãÎ bawling is a very effective way of getting attention from caregivers.' Then: 'Around adolescence, we begin to cry less over physical pain and more over emotional pain.' And: 'Many people also start to exhibit "moral crying", in reaction to acts of bravery, self-sacrifice and altruismãÎ as we age, we increasingly shed tears over things that are positive.'
steps.title: Locate the key sentences

steps.text: Boiled down: crying isn't one thing ã what triggers it changes with age. Babies cry to summon caregivers (physical). Adolescents cry more from emotional pain. Adults add 'moral crying' (in response to bravery, sacrifice). Older adults cry more at positive events (weddings, holidays). So the WHAT and the WHY of crying shift along a predictable life-stage path.
steps.title: Restate in plain English

steps.text: 'Encompasses' = includes, covers. 'Wailing' = loud crying-out. 'Mystery' = something not understood. 'Mortality' = the fact that we die; awareness of death. 'Moral crying' = the paragraph's own term for tears in response to ethical events (bravery, sacrifice). 'Predictable' in option C means 'follows a regular pattern' ã exactly what age-staged shifts describe.
steps.title: Vocabulary check

steps.text: Walk each option. A says very little is known about why crying is uniquely human ã but the paragraph DOES say a lot about why babies and adolescents cry; only the 'tears of joy' part is left as mystery, not the whole act. B says tears of joy have a different emotional origin from tears of sadness ã but the paragraph's THEORY says tears of joy may not reflect happiness at all; they may reflect bittersweet awareness of mortality, which is closer to sadness than to a separate origin. C says the characteristics of crying shift in predictable ways throughout life ã matches the baby ã adolescent ã adult ã ageing arc. D says whether or not people cry is culturally determined ã culture is never mentioned in the paragraph.
steps.title: Match against the options

steps.text: The answer is C. The paragraph's structural claim is a life-stage progression: babies, adolescents, adults, ageing ã each stage has its characteristic crying. C names that age-linked predictability; the other options either over-state mystery (A), invent a contrast (B), or import culture (D).
steps.title: Conclusion

var-2019-verb1-ELF-040.technique: Structure-of-claim ELF: when a paragraph describes a topic across LIFE STAGES (infants, adolescence, adulthood, ageing), the correct option names that AGE-LINKED PROGRESSION. Trigger: lists tied to age ã 'babiesãÎ adolescenceãÎ as we age' ã match options with words like 'throughout life', 'across the lifespan', 'in predictable ways'.

pregrade_tactic.move: When the text describes something across life stages (infant, youth, adult, old age), pick the option that names the age-bound development.

distractors.why_tempting: Det ûÊr frestande att tolka recensentens ûÑnskemûËl om motivering av tidsperioden som kritik mot att urvalet ûÊr fûÑr begrûÊnsat.
distractors.why_wrong: Hagelin saknar bara ett resonemang kring VARFûR just 1630-talet valts ã hon ifrûËgasûÊtter inte urvalets storlek. Som steg 5 visar ûÊr hennes ord om Falks tydlighet ("genomgûËende mycket noggrann") snarare en pluspoûÊng ûÊn kritik mot tillgûÊngligheten.

distractors.why_tempting: MûËnga stannar vid att texten nûÊmner "tidigare forskning" och tror att recensenten klagar pûË beroendet av andra studier.
distractors.why_wrong: Recensenten sûÊger raka motsatsen: Falks resultat "finner stûÑd i tidigare forskning men ger samtidigt en utûÑkad och uttûÑmmande bild". Tidigare forskning ûÊr alltsûË en bekrûÊftande ram, inte en krycka. Steg 5 fûËngar den vûÊndningen.

"GodkûÊnda" = accepterade (fel kontrast ã godkûÊnda resolutioner kan ûÊndûË vara normgivande). "Bindande" = juridiskt tvingande (exakt rûÊtt ã motsatsen till normgivande). Triggern: bûÑrja med den lucka dûÊr alternativen sprider sig mest tydligt (hûÊr lucka 3 ã "bindande" sticker ut som det enda juridiska tvûËngsordet), eliminera fûÑrst, lûÊs sedan kvar.

distractors.why_tempting: Det ûÊr frestande att tûÊnka pûË Disney-filmer som "grandiosa" ã storslagna produktioner med kûÊnda fûÑrlagor.

Lucka 1-ordet ska betyda "kommersiellt gûËngbar/sûÊljer biljetter". "Publikdragande" = drar publik (exakt rûÊtt). "Grandiosa" = storslagna (kan stûÊmma men sûÊger inget om fûÑrlagor). steps.title: Hitta begrûÊnsningen i lucka 2

steps.text: A "publikdragande ã utmanande": kommersiellt sûÊker + konstnûÊrligt vûËgad. Klassisk filmbranschkontrast. BûËda passar. B "grandiosa ã karaktûÊristiska": grandiost passar svagt om fûÑrlagebaserad, karaktûÊristiska ûÊr fûÑr diffust fûÑr en kontrast. C "utlûÊndska ã inhemska": fel axel ã kontrasten i texten ûÊr inte ursprungsland, utan kommersiellt vs konstnûÊrligt. D "animerade ã populistiska": populistiska ûÊr motsatsen till vad lucka 2 ska beskriva (barnens egen verklighet ûÊr personligt-realistiskt, inte populistiskt).
steps.title: Matcha mot alternativen

steps.text: Endast A klarar kontrasten: lucka 1 = sûÊljer biljetter via redan etablerad publik, lucka 2 = konstnûÊrligt djûÊrv men kommersiellt riskabel. Svaret ûÊr A.

var-2019-verb1-MEK-030.technique: Kontrastaxelregeln: nûÊr tvûË filmkategorier (eller tvûË grupper av nûËgot) stûÊlls mot varandra, leta efter den enda axel som meningens egna beskrivningar EXPLICIT pekar ut. distractors.why_tempting: Det ûÊr lûÊtt att gissa nûÊr ordet ûÊr obekant, och soppskûËl ûÊr ett konkret bordsfûÑremûËl ã kandelabern stûËr ocksûË ofta pûË matsalsbordet, sûË associationen 'fint bordsfûÑremûËl' kan landa pûË fel sak.

var-2019-verb1-ORD-001.solution_path: En kandelaber ûÊr en stor, grenad ljusstake ã den hûÑgtidliga sorten som stûËr pûË matsalsbordet eller i kyrkan. Svaret ûÊr E.

steps.text: En kandelaber ûÊr en stor ljusstake med flera armar som var och en bûÊr ett ljus ã bilden ûÊr den klassiska sjuarmade ljusstaken eller den hûÑga, grenade ljusbûÊraren pûË ett festligt middagsbord. Ordet bûÊr en hûÑgtidlig och nûËgot ûËlderdomlig klang; ingen ber sin granne om en kandelaber, man mûÑter ordet i kyrkor, slottsmiljûÑer eller romaner. Funktionen ûÊr densamma som hos vilken ljusstake som helst ã hûËlla ljus upprûÊtt ã men formatet ûÊr prakt-versionen.

steps.text: Ordet kommer frûËn latinets candela, 'vaxljus' ã samma stam som i kandelaber, kandelabra, och ûÊven i det engelska candle. Du ser den ocksûË i kandidat (ursprungligen nûËgon klûÊdd i candidus, 'skinande vit') och i kandera (att ûÑverdra med vit sockerglasyr). NûÊr du ser kand-/candi bûÑrjan, tûÊnk 'ljus / strûËlande / vitt' ã det leder rûÊtt hûÊr.

steps.text: Ljusstake (E) trûÊffar exakt ã det ûÊr det vanliga svenska ordet fûÑr fûÑremûËlet en kandelaber tillhûÑr. Kandelabern ûÊr en specifik, grenad och pûËkostad variant av ljusstaken, men kategorin ûÊr just ljusstake.

var-2019-verb1-ORD-001.technique: Latinska kand-/cand-stigen: nûÊr huvudordet bûÑrjar pûË kand- eller cand-, tûÊnk 'ljus, glans, vit' (av candela, 'vaxljus'). Triggern att memorera: 'kand-/cand- ã ljus' ã fungerar fûÑr kandelaber, kandidat, kandera och hjûÊlper utesluta allt som inte har med ljus att gûÑra.

distractors.why_tempting: Det ûÊr lûÊtt att lûÊsa 'under-' i undermûËlig som 'underordnad' eller 'kuvad' och dra slutsatsen att ordet beskriver nûËgon som blivit pressad ner ã alltsûË fûÑrtryckt.

distractors.why_wrong: 'MûËl' i undermûËlig betyder MûTT (standard), inte 'tal' eller 'rûÑst'. FûÑrstummad handlar om att inte kunna tala; undermûËlig handlar om att inte hûËlla mûËttet. TvûË olika 'mûËl' med samma stavning.

var-2019-verb1-ORD-002.solution_path: UndermûËlig betyder under mûËttet ã inte tillrûÊckligt bra, otillrûÊcklig i kvalitet. Svaret ûÊr D.

steps.text: UndermûËlig ûÊr ett kvalitetsomdûÑme: det betyder att nûËgot inte nûËr upp till den standard som krûÊvs eller fûÑrvûÊntas. En undermûËlig uppsats hûËller inte mûËttet; en undermûËlig produkt ûÊr fûÑr dûËlig att sûÊlja. Ordet ûÊr negativt laddat och ganska formellt ã det dyker upp i recensioner, utvûÊrderingar och myndighetstexter, sûÊllan i kompiskonversation. Det ûÊr en saklig, ofta lite strûÊng dom.
steps.title: Vad betyder undermûËlig?

steps.text: Ordet ûÊr genomskinligt byggt: under + mûËl, dûÊr 'mûËl' hûÊr betyder MûTT eller standardsnarare ûÊn 'syfte'. Samma 'mûËl' finns i mûËttstock, mûËttband och i uttrycket 'hûËlla mûËttet'. Bilden ûÊr en linje pûË en mûÊtsticka ã undermûËligt ligger under den linjen. Motsatsen vore ûÑvermûËlig (sûÊllsynt) eller helt enkelt fullgod / fullvûÊrdig.
steps.title: Under + mûËl = 'under mûËttet'

steps.text: BristfûÊllig (D) trûÊffar exakt ã det betyder 'som har brister', alltsûË inte komplett eller inte tillrûÊckligt bra. BûËda orden pekar pûË ett kvalitetsunderskott utan att specificera exakt vad som fattas; de ûÊr utbytbara i de flesta sammanhang.

var-2019-verb1-ORD-002.technique: Sammansatta ord med under-: nûÊr huvudordet ûÊr 'under + X', testa om 'under' anger 'fûÑr lite av X' / 'sûÊmre ûÊn X krûÊver'. HûÊr: under + mûËl (= mûËtt) ã 'under mûËttet' = otillrûÊcklig. Samma mûÑnster fungerar fûÑr undermûÊnsklig, underkûÊnd, underbemannad.

distractors.why_tempting: Det ûÊr lûÊtt att hûÑra 'feromoner' som nûËgot kemiskt-aromatiskt och associera till smaksûÊttare i mat ã bûËda ûÊr ju 'kemiska ûÊmnen som pûËverkar sinnena'.
distractors.why_wrong: SmaksûÊttare verkar via SMAKSINNET pûË tungan och tillsûÊtts medvetet i mat. Feromoner utsûÑndras NATURLIGT frûËn kroppen och verkar via lukt (eller andra kemiska sinnen) mellan individer. Helt olika sinnen och helt olika syfte.

distractors.why_tempting: FûÑrleden 'fer-' kan klinga som ett djurgruppsnamn ã som 'ferae' eller liknande latinska klassifikationer ã och leda tanken till nûÊsseldjur.

distractors.why_tempting: MûËnga stannar vid att 'fer-' lûËter biologiskt och vûÊxtliknande, och slingervûÊxter ûÊr ett biologiskt fackord som lûËter ungefûÊr lika exotiskt.
distractors.why_wrong: SlingervûÊxter ûÊr VûXTER ã alltsûË ocksûË levande organismer. Feromoner ûÊr inte organismer utan signalûÊmnen som organismer producerar. Det ûÊr som att fûÑrvûÊxla 'svett' med 'mûÊnniska' ã produkten ûÊr inte producenten.

distractors.why_tempting: Det ûÊr frestande att lûÊsa feromoner som nûËgot hormonliknande och kosttillskotts-aktigt ã alltsûË ett nûÊringspreparat man kan svûÊlja.
distractors.why_wrong: NûÊringspreparat tas IN fûÑr att bygga upp kroppen frûËn insidan. Feromoner slûÊpps UT frûËn kroppen fûÑr att signalera till andra. Riktningen ûÊr motsatt ã in mot kroppen kontra ut frûËn kroppen ã och syftet ûÊr nûÊring kontra kommunikation.

var-2019-verb1-ORD-003.solution_path: Feromoner ûÊr kemiska doftsignaler som djur (och mûÊnniskor) utsûÑndrar fûÑr att kommunicera ã locka, varna, markera revir. Svaret ûÊr E.

steps.text: Feromoner ûÊr osynliga kemiska signalûÊmnen som djur utsûÑndrar fûÑr att skicka meddelanden till andra individer av samma art ã 'kom hit, jag ûÊr hona', 'fara, fly', 'det hûÊr ûÊr mitt revir'. De kûÊnns frûÊmst genom luktsinnet (eller mer subtila sinnesorgan hos insekter). Du har hûÑrt ordet i samband med myror som lûÊgger doftspûËr, eller hundar som markerar med urin, eller diskussionen om huruvida mûÊnniskor pûËverkas av andras kroppsdofter.
steps.title: Vad ûÊr feromoner?

steps.text: Ordet ûÊr konstruerat av forskare ur grekiskan: pherein (bûÊra) + hormon (vûÊcka, sûÊtta i rûÑrelse). En feromon 'bûÊr ett hormon' ã alltsûË bûÊr ett kemiskt budskap mellan individer (till skillnad frûËn hormoner som bara verkar inom kroppen). Samma pherein-stam finns i metafor (det som BûRS ûÑver frûËn ett omrûËde till ett annat) och i periferi.
steps.title: Grekiska pherein ã 'bûÊra'

steps.text: DoftûÊmnen (E) ûÊr den exakta svenska ûÑversûÊttningen: kemiska ûÊmnen som verkar genom doft. Det ûÊr inte en perfekt synonym tekniskt (alla doftûÊmnen ûÊr inte feromoner), men det ûÊr det enda alternativet som fûËngar bûËde den kemiska och den luktbaserade naturen av feromoner.
steps.title: VûÊlj synonymen

var-2019-verb1-ORD-003.technique: Grekiska -on-suffixet i naturvetenskap: nûÊr ett ord slutar pûË -on (feromon, hormon, neuron, elektron) handlar det nûÊstan alltid om en LITEN ENHET inom biologi/fysik (en molekyl, en cell, en partikel). distractors.why_tempting: Det ûÊr lûÊtt att hûÑra 'ex-' i exponera och tûÊnka 'extra' eller 'ûÑverdriva' ã alltsûË blûËsa upp nûËgot fûÑr effekt.

distractors.why_tempting: MûËnga stannar vid att 'visa upp' och 'fûÑretrûÊda' bûËda handlar om att stûË fram offentligt, sûË fûÑretrûÊda kan kûÊnnas som en bra match.

steps.text: Att exponera ûÊr att VISA UPP nûËgot eller lûËta det utsûÊttas fûÑr nûËgon yttre pûËverkan. I marknadsfûÑring exponeras varan i skyltfûÑnstret ã visas upp fûÑr fûÑrbipasserande. I medicin exponeras huden fûÑr UV-ljus ã utsûÊtts fûÑr strûËlning. PûË fotot kallas det exponering nûÊr ljus slûÊpps in pûË fil- men. Den rûÑda trûËden ûÊr 'gûÑra nûËgot synligt eller ûËtkomligt fûÑr en yttre kraft (blickar, ljus, ûÊmnen)'.
steps.title: Vad betyder exponera?

steps.text: Ordet ûÊr byggt av latinskt ex- (ut) + ponere (stûÊlla, lûÊgga) ã alltsûË 'stûÊlla ut'. Samma ponere-stam finns i komponera (stûÊlla samman), deponera (lûÊgga ner), proponera (lûÊgga fram). NûÊr du ser pon-/posi ett ord, tûÊnk 'placera' ã och 'ex-' framfûÑr betyder att det placeras UT i ljuset, i offentligheten, i farozonen.

steps.text: UtsûÊtta (B) trûÊffar kûÊrnan: att placera nûËgon eller nûËgot i en position dûÊr en yttre pûËverkan fûËr verka. 'UtsûÊtta' rûËkar betona faro-aspekten, vilket exponera ocksûË ofta gûÑr i moderna sammanhang.

var-2019-verb1-ORD-004.technique: Latinska pon-/pos-stigen: nûÊr huvudordet innehûËller -pon- eller -pos- (exponera, deponera, komponera, position), tûÊnk 'placera' och frûËga vilken riktning prefixet anger. ex- ã ut; de- ã ner; kom- ã samman. distractors.why_tempting: Det ûÊr lûÊtt att fûÑrvûÊxla uppsûËt med impuls eftersom bûËda fûÑregûËr en handling och bûËda kan kûÊnnas som nûËgot 'inifrûËn' personen.

distractors.why_tempting: MûËnga stannar vid att uppsûËt och drivkraft bûËda handlar om vad som fûËr nûËgon att handla ã alltsûË motivationen.

distractors.why_tempting: Snabbsvar ûÊr ofta 'tillsyn' om man lûÊser 'upp-' som en uppifrûËn-blick och 'sûËt' som nûËgot att hûËlla ûÑga pûË.
